name: Pariacaca
description: Pariacaca was a significant pre-Inca and Inca deity associated with mountains, water, storms, and agriculture in the central Andes region of Peru. He was considered one of the most important deities in the Huarochirí province, located east of Lima. Pariacaca originated as a falcon (or five falcons, representing five brothers) and transformed into a powerful human figure capable of controlling weather and landscapes. He was a rival to other deities, particularly Huallallo Carhuincho, whom he defeated in a legendary battle for dominance. This victory established Pariacaca as the primary deity of the region and solidified his association with the fertile highlands and the irrigation systems vital for agriculture. Pariacaca's power was manifested through thunderstorms, floods, and the control of water resources, making him both feared and revered. Offerings and rituals were performed to appease him and ensure bountiful harvests. Stories about Pariacaca emphasize his transformative abilities, his role as a cultural hero who brought order and prosperity to the land, and his enduring importance in the religious beliefs of the people of Huarochirí and beyond. The deity embodies the power and unpredictability of nature, as well as the vital connection between the mountains, water, and the sustenance of life.
subtitle: Mountain God of Storms and Water

keyMyths.title: Pariacaca and Huallallo Carhuincho
keyMyths.description: Pariacaca battled Huallallo Carhuincho for supremacy, ultimately defeating him with rain and floods. This victory established Pariacaca as the dominant deity of the region.

keyMyths.title: The Five Falcons
keyMyths.description: Pariacaca originated as five falcons who transformed into a powerful human figure. Each falcon represents a brother with unique powers.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Irrigation
keyMyths.description: Pariacaca is credited with creating irrigation systems to provide water for agriculture. His control over water was essential for the prosperity of the people.

sources.author: Ávila, Francisco de
sources.work: The Huarochirí Manuscript: A Testament of Ancient and Colonial Andean Religion
sources.description: A key primary source documenting the mythology and religious beliefs of the people of Huarochirí, including detailed accounts of Pariacaca.
sources.text: A key primary source documenting the mythology and religious beliefs of the people of Huarochirí, including detailed accounts of Pariacaca.
